episode_title: Adrian Gill: How AI Image Generation is Revolutionizing the Graphic Design Industry

codes.description: The principle that AI should be used like Photoshop -- leveraged as a tool but never visibly apparent in the final output; the creator's vision must remain primary.
excerpts: It's important, you know, for my work not to let AI be visible. It's like, for example, everybody uses Photoshop. But you don't want your images to look photoshopped. Right? So it's the same principle where you can leverage AI, but at the end of the day, your own vision has to be front and foremost.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Written and visual communications work hand in hand; writing teams need visual cues and design teams need written content, making AI's impact on either domain relevant to the other.
excerpts: Written and visual communications go hand in hand. There's so many times when I'm working with a design team and they want our writers. to write us something that we can build on and vice versa.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI compresses the ideation timeline dramatically, making two days of brainstorming feel like two weeks of work.
excerpts: if you're ideating maybe two days of ideating, it really looks like two weeks. of ideating. Right? So it can be a very, very good tool if you're strategic about it and you're using it with the right motivation.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.code: copyright and IP as primary concern
codes.description: The dominant concern around image generation tools is intellectual property and copyright infringement since these tools scrape millions of existing images.
excerpts: Most of the concern around those things are around intellectual property and copyright infringement and stuff like this. So that's the qualifier from the very beginning because these tools are scraping millions of images, for example, from everywhere and it comes from images that were already created by someone.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: The input to AI image tools should come from a creative vision or future-oriented idea, not from prompting the tool blindly; the tool fills in spots and accelerates but does not originate the vision.
excerpts: the input needs to come from a vision, let's say, or some sort of future-oriented thinking of an idea. that you would like to explore and then you leverage the the tools to maybe help you fill in some spots or get to some place quicker
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI helps process and find themes in large volumes of design inspiration images (e.g., 1000+ photos from research trips) -- a task previously near-impossible at that scale.
excerpts: But then two weeks later when you get back to your desk. Now what do you do with over a thousand images? How can you find a theme? Like what did you see? What did you inadvertently or you're inadvertently drawn to in terms of the photos that you took? So being able to drop something like that into a mid-journey definitely might help pull themes.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI-generated images are fundamentally based on things already created, meaning they cannot produce truly novel outputs without human creative intervention.
excerpts: All of these images are based on things that have been created in the past. So if you just rely on this solely, you're gonna get maybe something that's interesting, but if it's all been done before, then is it really new? That's where your ingenuity, our ingenuity as humans come in to really create something that is distinctive.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI image tools help non-designers communicate their vision to designers by generating visual references rather than relying solely on written briefs.
excerpts: I think it also helps non-designers, maybe talk to designers and have that conversation make it easier
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Client briefs with AI-generated visual references are more meaningful than text-only documents because designers are visual people who process written briefs less effectively.
excerpts: So many times I see design breeze from clients that are just a long document with words. And sometimes these words sound very much like other words from other clients with just a different product in mind. And so for a designer, those things almost become things that filter in but can be ignored in some ways
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: When clients provide AI-generated visual references, designers must retain the freedom to depart from that initial vision, otherwise the design process loses its value.
excerpts: It's important though that when that's done, that the designer then gets the freedom to depart from that initial vision. So That is the tricky part because if then you require a designer to come back with something that looks exactly like what you gave them, then Why are you talking, right?
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI-generated imagery tends toward the literal and obvious, requiring human creative judgment to rework outputs into something more nuanced and original.
excerpts: Some of them were very literal. Okay. Right? In which case we reworked them. Put in the additional imagery to help move it into a space that was not so literal.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: At the Harvard President's Innovation Challenge, AI-generated imagery was shown without disclosure or attribution, following the principle that design should be felt rather than explained.
excerpts: we didn't say anything about it. Okay. We just showed it. And you know, audience engagement is a big part of what we do. So sometimes you want people to feel things without being told.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: The question of who to credit for AI-generated images is unresolved: not Midjourney itself, but the original artists whose work was scraped, making clean attribution impossible.
excerpts: who do you credit? Because it's not mid journey, right? It's some other artist that has created something that Midjourney has pulled from.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Professional designers create original work first, feed it into AI tools, then use the output to iterate further -- a creative loop where AI provides future inputs that never existed before.
excerpts: they create something first, drop that into the mid-journey, and Then that creates something which allows them to maybe think a little bit more about and add new prompts to develop their work further. So in that case, that's a future input that's something that's not existed before
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: The fundamental role of the designer does not change with AI; what changes is speed, volume, and range of outputs.
excerpts: I don't see the role of the designer changing that much. But I see the inputs, I see different outputs, I see how, you know, the speed at which things are done, the volume of information that can be collated in a short space of time.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Different designers will use AI very differently: some will push many creative inputs, others will use it efficiently, some will refuse it, and others will experiment with entirely new worlds.
excerpts: some designers might be much more creative about how they approach it. Others might see it as a tool and really be very efficient in how they use that tool. Some people might choose not to use it at all.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: The impact of AI on design and journalism is structurally identical: both require human judgment, editorial/creative thinking, and are threatened by the same automation pressures.
excerpts: if you just replace everything that you say with the word journalist, it would make more sense also for the journalism industry. Like every single thing you're saying is the same thing with the design industry.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: At least 50% of journalists in workshops have never tried image generation tools, a significantly lower experimentation rate than with text generation tools.
excerpts: image generation is one place where I see a lot of people have not even trying it out. They are not even experimenting with it. At least 50% of the people would not even try it.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Journalists avoid image generation tools not from lack of interest but from ethical discomfort, perceiving the tools as inherently unethical due to copyright concerns.
excerpts: it just feels unethical to a lot of people. That that's what they usually tell me. That's why they don't want to even try out these tools.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Organizations that use AI-generated content without a distinctive creative point of view risk undifferentiated output that blends into generic sameness.
excerpts: are you just trying to get something out quickly and efficiently and you don't mind if it sits down into the sea of sameness.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: How effectively AI image generation is used depends on organizational mindset -- whether the organization values innovation, creativity, and differentiation versus speed and cost savings.
excerpts: it comes down to mindset. What is the mindset of the organization? Is it an innovative organization? Is it a creative organization? Do you want your editors to be creative about this or are you just trying to get something out quickly
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Nikita has met people directly told they were replaced by AI, framing this as mismanagement rather than inevitable technological progress.
excerpts: I've had to meet people who have lost jobs in the industry. And some people have come and told me that they were directly told that they were replaced by AI. And I think that's mismanagement and leaders not envisioning or understanding the role of AI properly.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.code: expanding the pie not replacing workers
codes.description: AI should be used to get more work done (expand the pie) rather than to replace workers and get the same amount done; the latter is not a win-win.
excerpts: you should be expanding the pie basically, getting more work done like with AI not like replacing and getting the same amount of work done, that doesn't seem to be a win win situation for any company.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: The best defense against AI displacement for designers is to perfect their craft, maintain an innovative mindset, and develop a clear point of view -- because generic inputs give reasons to replace them.
excerpts: What I'd say to designers is perfect your craft. Continue to look and gather your sources of inspiration and keep an innovative mindset. I think that future perspective is important. Having a a clearer point of view as you design is really, really important. Because if your inputs are just par for the course, then that gives persons reasons to just use AI and not use designers.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: AI should be framed as collaboration with human creativity, not competition against it; the future is about limitless potential through human-AI partnership.
excerpts: I think it's really about collaboration versus competition. And at the end of the day, if it can help give you a a new idea or make something just a little bit better. I I think that is fair.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Concrete example of AI compressing a creative production timeline from weeks to days for the Harvard Innovation Challenge video project.
excerpts: that process, if we were to try to do it or cells would have taken weeks and it took days, right?
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Fink Media has built image generation directly into their CMS, allowing editors to prompt images for articles without leaving their publishing workflow.
excerpts: Fink Media have built it out in their CMS itself where editors can go and for a particular article they can like prompt it and prompt an image that they can then use within their news articles.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: The possibility of fine-tuning image generation models on a brand's existing illustration style (like Axios) to maintain visual consistency while automating production.
excerpts: you can do fine-tuning the way you fine-tune large language models. You're fine-tuning like all of these image generation models based on inspiration of past illustrations to start spitting things out in that direction.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.code: forward-thinking vs. collage of the past
codes.description: Organizations must choose between being forward-thinking design leaders that others follow, or becoming a collage of the past with small tweaks.
excerpts: do you want to be part of collage of the past with small tweaks? Or do you want to be a part of future more innovative design direction? and have others follow you.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Optimistic framing that humans 'breathe life into technologies' and that applying imagination makes possibilities endless.
excerpts: As humans, we breathe life into these technologies. As long as we apply imagination or imaginations, then I think the possibilities are endless and the future is quite bright.
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

codes.description: Even outside professional contexts, using AI for personal creative projects (like sneaker design) builds understanding and fuels professional creative practice.
excerpts: I have dabbled in some sneaker design using my inputs for I keep hammering on this input stuff, but it's true, you know, like what would it look like if we could do this or that?
codes.speaker: Adrian Gill

episode_summary: This episode brings an outside-of-journalism perspective through Adrian Gill, a design industry veteran, revealing that the AI disruption anxieties in journalism and design are structurally identical: both professions face the same automation pressures, both require human judgment to elevate AI outputs from generic to distinctive, and both must contend with copyright and attribution complexities. Gill's central argument is that AI image generation is fundamentally backward-looking (trained on past work) and therefore incapable of true novelty without human creative intervention, positioning the designer's role as enduringly necessary even as workflows dramatically accelerate. The conversation surfaces a notable tension around disclosure -- Gill chose not to disclose AI-generated imagery at a Harvard event, framing non-disclosure as consistent with design philosophy ('you don't want your images to look photoshopped'), which sits uncomfortably alongside journalism's emerging transparency norms. The episode also reveals a significant adoption gap, with at least 50% of journalists never having tried image generation tools, largely due to ethical discomfort rather than lack of access.
surprises: Gill did not disclose the use of AI-generated imagery at the Harvard President's Innovation Challenge, framing this as consistent with design philosophy rather than as a transparency failure -- a stance that directly conflicts with journalistic norms.
His observation that AI-generated images are often too literal and obvious challenges the common perception that AI creativity is impressively sophisticated.

The admission that written design briefs are often 'ignored' by designers because they all sound the same suggests AI-generated visual briefs could fundamentally restructure client-designer communication.
The Newsroom Robots podcast logo itself was created using Midjourney as a starting point, making the podcast's own production a case study in the topic it covers.
tensions: Non-disclosure of AI-generated imagery is presented as acceptable design practice, while the podcast's broader context is journalism where AI transparency is increasingly demanded -- creating a cross-disciplinary ethical friction.
The attribution dilemma is acknowledged but unresolved: you cannot credit Midjourney because the real creators are the scraped artists, yet those artists receive no credit either.

Gill argues AI does not change the designer's role while simultaneously describing dramatically different workflows, compressed timelines, and new creative loops -- suggesting the role changes more than acknowledged.
The 'expanding the pie' aspiration (use AI to do more, not replace people) conflicts with the testified reality that people have already been directly replaced by AI.
Ethical reluctance keeps 50%+ of journalists from even trying image generation tools, creating a knowledge gap that may leave them less prepared for a future where these tools are ubiquitous.
